coredata.dc:title: Design and analysis of an inductor-less cross-coupled voltage equalizer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: 2020 The 7th International Conference on Power and Energy Systems Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Eguchi, Kei
Shibata, Akira
Do, Wanglok
Asadi, Farzin
coredata.dc:description: To equalize the imbalance in the state-of-charge (SoC) of lithium-ion cells, we propose a novel inductor-less cross-coupled voltage equalizer for series-connected batteries. Unlike existing symmetrical voltage equalizers using diode switches, the proposed voltage equalizer can balance the voltage of battery cells without a threshold voltage drop, because the diode-less design is realized by the cross-coupled structure. The performance of the proposed voltage equalizer is clarified by theoretical analysis, computer simulations, and breadboard-based experimental tests. The computer simulations and experiments reveal that the proposed voltage equalizer outperforms conventional inductor-less voltage equalizers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Voltage equalizers
Cross-coupled structure
Symmetrical structure
Inductor-less circuits
Battery cells

T
2
.
Simulated results: (a) Output voltage; (b) Power efficiency.
Measured output voltage in a steady state: (a) Proposed SCVE; (b) Conventional SCVE [6].

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2020 7th International Conference on Power and Energy Systems Engineering (CPESE 2020), 26–29​ September 2020, Fukuoka, Japan

head.ce:title: Design and analysis of an inductor-less cross-coupled voltage equalizer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Information Electronics, Fukuoka Institute of Technology, 3-30-1 Wajirohigashi, Higashi-ku, Fukuoka, 811-0295, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Information Electronics, Fukuoka Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Information Electronics, Fukuoka Institute of Technology, 3-30-1 Wajirohigashi, Higashi-ku, Fukuoka, 811-0295 Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, Maltepe University, Istanbul, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, Maltepe University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, Maltepe University, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To equalize the imbalance in the state-of-charge (SoC) of lithium-ion cells, we propose a novel inductor-less cross-coupled voltage equalizer for series-connected batteries. Unlike existing symmetrical voltage equalizers using diode switches, the proposed voltage equalizer can balance the voltage of battery cells without a threshold voltage drop, because the diode-less design is realized by the cross-coupled structure. The performance of the proposed voltage equalizer is clarified by theoretical analysis, computer simulations, and breadboard-based experimental tests. The computer simulations and experiments reveal that the proposed voltage equalizer outperforms conventional inductor-less voltage equalizers.

ce:section.ce:para: In mobile electronics applications, lithium-ion batteries with multiple cells are commonly used to meet high voltage requirements. However, the imbalance in the state-of-charge (SoC) of lithium-ion cells is a big issue [1] in the series-connected batteries. To solve this problem, an inductor-less voltage equalizer designed by switched-capacitor (SC) techniques [2,3] is one of the most promising circuits. Past studies have proposed several types of switched-capacitor voltage equalizers (SCVEs), such as star-structured SCVE [4], mesh-structured SCVE [5], and symmetrical SCVE [6]. Among others, the symmetrical SCVE [6] is a state-of-the-art circuit that can balance the voltage of battery cells effectively. However, in the symmetrical SCVE [6], the cell voltage decreases by the threshold voltage of diode switches.

In this paper, a novel inductor-less cross-coupled voltage equalizer is proposed for series-connected batteries. Unlike the symmetrical SCVE [6], the proposed SCVE requires no diode switch. By cross-coupling CMOS switches, the diode-less design is realized in the proposed SCVE. Therefore, the proposed SCVE can evenly balance the voltage of battery cells without a threshold voltage drop. To clarify the effectiveness of the proposed SCVE, theoretical analysis, computer simulations, and experiments are conducted regarding the SCVEs with four battery cells.

The conventional symmetrical SCVE [6] with four battery cells is illustrated in Fig. 1. To equalize the voltage of the battery cell BT
i
(
i = 1
, …, 4),
V
B T i
, the diode switches are driven by the two-phase clock pulses Φ
1 and Φ
2 with amplitude
V
i n
. The instantaneous equivalent circuits of the conventional SCVE are expressed as Fig. 2, where
V
t h
is the threshold voltage of the diode. As it can be seen Fig. 2,
V
B T i
decreases due to
V
t h
.

The proposed SCVE with four battery cells is illustrated in Fig. 3. Unlike, the conventional SCVE shown in Fig. 1, the proposed SCVE employs CMOS cross-coupled switches [7,8] instead of diode switches. In Fig. 3, the CMOS switch is driven by the following gate voltage
V
g
:
(1)
For
M
n i
and
M
p i
:
V
g
=
V
i + 4
+
V
i n
2
i f
Φ
2
i s
O n ,

i + 4
−
V
i n
2
i f
Φ
2
i s
O f f .
For
M
n i + 4
and
M
p i + 4
:
V
g
=
V
i
+
V
i n
2
i f
Φ
1
i s
O n ,
V
i
−
V
i n
2
i f
Φ
1
i s
O f f .

Since the CMOS switch is fully turned on and off by
V
g
, the proposed SCVE can suppress the voltage drop.

For calculating the output voltage of the proposed SCVE shown in Fig. 3, theoretical analysis is performed by utilizing the four-terminal equivalent model [9,10] shown in Fig. 4. In this figure,
m
1
is the conversion ratio of the ideal transformer and
R
S C 1
is the internal resistance. In the following analysis, we obtain
m
1
and
R
S C 1
by assuming the conditions: i) on-resistance of all transistor switches is
R
o n
and ii) time constant is much bigger than the period of the inputs. Under these assumptions, the instantaneous equivalent circuits of the proposed SCVE can be represented by Fig. 5. In this figure,

(

.

Since the sum of the variations in the electric charge of capacitors is zero in the cycle T, the relationship between the input currents,
I
i n 1
and
I

, and the output current,

, are obtained as

is obtained by considering the consumed energy of Fig. 5. Since the instantaneous equivalent circuits shown in Fig. 5 have a symmetrical structure, the total consumed energy
W

of the four-terminal equivalent model is given as

Finally, by combining

and

, the equivalent model of the proposed SCVE is expressed as

.
Therefore, we have the output voltage of the proposed SCVE as

o u t

where
R
L
is an output load.

In the computer simulations, the comparison between the proposed SCVE and the conventional SCVE [6] is conducted by using the Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis (SPICE) simulator. In the performed simulations, the parameters were set to
V
i n
= 3
.7 V at 1 MHz,
R
o n
= 0 . 1 Ω
,
C
1
= … =
C
8
= 3 . 3
μ
F, and the capacity of all battery cells was set to 470 μ F. Fig. 6 shows the simulated results. As it can be seen from Fig. 6, the proposed SCVE can significantly improve the output voltage and power efficiency. It is noteworthy that about 25% power efficiency was improved by the proposed SCVE.

In the experiments, the breadboard circuit was assembled with discrete circuit components, where the battery BT
i
is assumed as a 470 μ F electrolytic capacitor. Fig. 7 demonstrates the measured output voltage when
V
i n
is 3.7 V at 400 Hz and
C
j
(
j = 1
, …, 8) is 3.3 μ F. In this figure,
V
o i
(
i = 1
, …, 4) denotes the voltage between the positive terminal of BT
i
and ground. Obviously, the proposed SCVE can achieve not only higher voltage of the battery cells but also smaller error than the conventional SCVE [6]. Concretely, the average cell voltage of the proposed SCVE reaches to 3.596 V with a standard deviation of 76.1 mV. On the other hand, the average cell voltage of the proposed SCVE is 2.999 V with a standard deviation of 90.5 mV.

In this paper, an inductor-less cross-coupled SCVE has been proposed for mobile electronics applications. In the case of four battery cells, the effectiveness of the proposed SCVE was confirmed by theoretical analysis, computer simulations, and experiments. In the performed circuit verification, the proposed SCVE is superior to the conventional state-of-the-art SCVE, where the average voltage of the battery cells was 3.596 V with a standard deviation of 76.1 mV.
In a future study, we plan to integrate the proposed SCVE into an IC chip and investigate it experimentally.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Kelkar Apoorva, Dasari Yashwanth, Williamson S. Sheldon, A Comprehensive Review of Power Electronics Enabled Active Battery Cell Balancing for Smart Energy Management. In: 2020 IEEE international conference on power electronics, smart grid and renewable energy; 2020. pp. 1–6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of parallel connected fibonacci switched capacitor converter

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Do Wanglok, Eguchi Kei. (2020) Analysis of parallel connected Fibonacci switched capacitor converter. Energy Reports 6 (2) 2020 : 362-367.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An analytical approach for design of a cross-connected fibonacci switched capacitor converter

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Do Wanglok, Bevrani Hassan, Shafiee Qobad, Eguchi Kei. (2020) An Analytical Approach for Design of a Cross-Connected Fibonacci Switched Capacitor Converter. Energies 13 (2) 2020 : 431.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Shang Yunlong, Xia Bing, Lu Fei, Zhang Chenghui, Cui Naxin, Wang Chunyu, Mi Chris, A star-structured switched-capacitor equalizer for series-connected battery strings. In: 2017 IEEE energy conversion congress and exposition; 2017. pp. 2723–27.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An optimized mesh-structured switched-capacitor equalizer for lithium-ion battery strings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shang Yunlong, Zhang Qi, Cui Naxin, Duan Bin, Zhang Chenghui. (2019) An Optimized Mesh-Structured Switched-Capacitor Equalizer for Lithium-Ion Battery Strings. IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification, 5 (1) 2019 : 252-261.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis and design of a voltage equalizer based on boost full-bridge inverter and symmetrical voltage multiplier for series-connected batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang Xiaoguang, Xi Ligen, Gao Zheng, Li Yuqi, Wen Jing. Analysis and Design of a Voltage Equalizer Based on Boost Full-Bridge Inverter and Symmetrical Voltage Multiplier for Series-Connected Batteries. IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology, 69 (4) 2020 : 3828-3840.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of an inductor-less step-up ac/dc converter for 0.3 V@1 MHz vibration energy harvesting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Eguchi Kei, Shibata Akira, Kuwahara Kyoka, Ishibashi Takaaki. (2020) Design of an inductor-less step-up ac/dc converter for 0.3 V@1 MHz vibration energy harvesting. Energy Reports 6 (2) 2020 : 159-165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Charge pump improvement for energy harvesting applications by node pre-charging
IEEE Trans Circuits Syst II: Express Briefs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ballo Andrea, Grasso Dario Alfio, Palumbo Gaetano. (2020) Charge Pump Improvement for Energy Harvesting Applications by Node Pre-Charging. IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs, (Early Access), (2020): 1-1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of an LED lighting circuit using a hybrid buck-boost converter with high gain

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Eguchi Kei, Kuwahara Kyoka, Ishibashi Takaaki. (2020) Analysis of an LED lighting circuit using a hybrid buck-boost converter with high gain. Energy Reports 6 (2) 2020 : 250-256.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A step-down nesting-type ac–ac converter combined with voltage equalizers and switched-capacitor simple converters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Eguchi Kei, Rubpongse Ratanaubol, Shibata Akira, Ishibashi Takaaki, Asadi Farzin. (2020) A step-down nesting-type ac–ac converter combined with voltage equalizers and switched-capacitor simple converters. Energy Reports 6 (2) 2020 : 173-178.
